title: Build Your First Agent
tagline: Build an autonomous agent that takes actions on your behalf. Not a chatbot. A doer.
outcome: When you finish this project you will have a working agent that perceives, decides, and acts on its own. Point it at a real folder or task, hit run, and watch it do work you used to do by hand. You will also have the pattern that every future agent you build will follow.
submission_prompt: Describe your agent in one sentence and paste a snippet from its log showing one full run. Optionally include a link to the repo or gist.

steps.title: Pick what your agent will do
steps.body:
A chatbot answers questions. An agent does things. It reads files, makes decisions, calls APIs, and keeps going until the job is done. Chatbot: *What's the weather?* Agent: *Check the weather every morning and text me if it will rain.*

Pick something repetitive that you actually do: a file organizer, a research summarizer, an email drafter, a data cleaner, a morning briefing builder. The best first agent is simple, useful, and low-consequence if it makes a mistake.

Write it down: *My agent will ___.* That is your North Star for the next six steps.

**Good first agents:** sort your Downloads folder by file type. Summarize unread emails into a one-line digest. Rename screenshots with a timestamp and a description.

**Bad first agents:** anything that sends messages or spends money without review. Anything that requires nuanced human judgment. Anything that touches production data.

**Why scope matters so much.** Beginners consistently pick agents that are too large. "An agent that manages my whole inbox" is three separate agents minimum — one that reads, one that categorizes, one that drafts replies. Starting large means debugging three things simultaneously when something breaks. Starting small means you ship something working in ninety minutes and can compose agents later.

The way to scope correctly is to ask: what is the single action I most want automated? Not the whole workflow. The one thing inside the workflow that takes disproportionate time or attention. If you do it more than five times per week and it follows a pattern, it is a good candidate.

**Test your choice.** Before moving on, check your target against three questions. First: can I describe every step the agent needs to take in plain English in under two minutes? If not, it is too complex. Second: is every step deterministic — does the same input always produce the same output? If the answer is "it depends," you need to write down what it depends on before an agent can handle it. Third: is the downside of a mistake recoverable? A file moved to the wrong folder is recoverable. An email sent to the wrong person is not. Start with recoverable.

**How to tell Claude what you picked:** Open Claude and say: *I am building my first agent. It will [one sentence describing the task]. I want to walk through the design before writing any code. Help me refine the scope if it sounds too large.* Claude will ask clarifying questions that surface hidden complexity early.
steps.done_when: You have one sentence that says exactly what your agent will do and what it will act on.

steps.title: Map the agent loop on paper
steps.body:
Every agent runs the same loop: **perceive, decide, act, repeat.** Before you ask Claude to write a single line of code, map these four steps for your agent.

- **Perceive:** what does it look at? File names, file contents, inbox, a spreadsheet, an API response.
- **Decide:** what are the rules? *If extension is .pdf, target Documents. If file has no extension, target Other.*
- **Act:** what does it do? Move a file, write a draft, update a row, log a line.
- **Repeat:** when does it stop? When the folder is empty, when a counter runs out, when you kill it.

**Edge cases are where agents break.** Define them now: what happens if a file has no extension? What if two files have the same name? What if the input is malformed? What should the agent NEVER do? Write these rules down before you touch the keyboard.

**How to map perceive.** List every piece of information the agent needs to make a decision. For a file organizer: file name, file extension, file creation date, file size. For an email summarizer: sender, subject, body text, date received. If the agent needs information it cannot access directly, that is a constraint you need to account for now. Do not discover it when the agent is running.

**How to map decide.** Decision logic is the heart of the agent. Write it as if-then rules: *If extension is .pdf → move to Documents. If extension is .jpg or .png → move to Images. If no extension → move to Review and log a warning.* Be exhaustive. Every case the agent might encounter needs a rule. Anything not explicitly covered becomes undefined behavior — which means the agent will guess, and it will guess wrong at the worst time.

**How to map act.** For each decision outcome, write the exact action: not "put it somewhere" but "move the file to ~/Documents/Sorted/[Year]/". Precision here eliminates a whole class of bugs. The agent does exactly what you describe, nothing more.

**How to map repeat.** Agents need a clear termination condition. Without one, they either run forever or stop too early. For a folder organizer: stop when every file in the source folder has been processed. For a summarizer: stop when every unread email has a summary entry. Write the condition explicitly.

**The prompt to use.** After you have your loop mapped on paper, paste it into Claude with this message: *Here is my agent loop. Perceive: [list]. Decide: [rules]. Act: [actions]. Repeat: [condition]. Edge cases: [list]. Does anything look incomplete or contradictory before we design the code?* Claude will catch conflicts you missed and ask about cases you did not think of. This conversation is the most valuable fifteen minutes in the whole project.
steps.done_when: You have your four loop steps and at least three edge case rules written down.

steps.title: Set up the project
steps.body:
Open your terminal. Create a folder for your agent. Start Claude Code inside it.

If you did Project 1, you already have Claude Code installed. If not, install it with npm first.

Your agent will be a small Python or JavaScript script. You do not need to know the language. Claude Code handles the install and setup. Your job is to describe what you want.

Once Claude is running, paste your sentence from Step 1 and your loop map from Step 2. Ask Claude to check your environment (Python version, dependencies) and set up a virtual environment.

**What to paste into Claude to start.** Use this prompt verbatim: *I am building an agent in this folder. Here is what it does: [your one-sentence description from Step 1]. Here is the loop I mapped: Perceive: [your list]. Decide: [your rules]. Act: [your actions]. Repeat: [your condition]. Edge cases: [your list]. Before we write any code, check my environment, recommend a language (Python or JavaScript), and list any libraries we will need. Then set up a virtual environment if Python, or initialize a package.json if JavaScript.*

**What Claude will do.** It will check whether Python and pip are installed, recommend Python for most agent tasks (better library support for file operations and scheduling), create a virtual environment, and list dependencies. You do not need to type a single install command yourself. Claude writes the commands and explains what each one does.

**What to verify before moving on.** Ask Claude: *List the exact Python version, the virtual environment path, and every installed library so I can confirm we are set up correctly.* Read the list. If anything looks unexpected, ask Claude to explain it before proceeding. A misconfigured environment causes mysterious failures later that are much harder to debug than a setup question now.

**Folder structure.** Ask Claude to create a standard folder structure: *Create the folder structure for this project with a main agent script, a logs folder, a test-data folder, and a README that describes what the agent does.* This structure will feel like overhead now and save you an hour of confusion later.

**What good setup looks like.** When you are done: the virtual environment is active (you will see its name in your terminal prompt), Claude can run a simple test command without errors, the folder structure exists, and you have a README with the agent's one-sentence description. Do not proceed to the design step until all four are true.
steps.done_when: Claude Code is running in your agent folder and has confirmed the environment is ready.

code.value:
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
mkdir my-agent
cd my-agent
claude

steps.title: Design the agent before building it
steps.body:
Paste your loop and edge cases into Claude Code. Then say: *Before writing any code, describe the tools the agent will use, the decision logic, and how it handles each edge case. I want to review the design first.*

Claude will come back with a plan: what functions it will write, what rules map to what actions, how errors get handled. Compare line by line to your map.

If something is missing (like *what about duplicates?*), flag it now. Fixing a design takes 30 seconds. Fixing code takes 30 minutes.

**One non-negotiable rule to add:** *The agent must never delete anything. It can move, rename, skip, or log, but never delete.* This protects you when edge cases surprise the agent.

**What a good design response looks like.** Claude should describe: the main function and what it accepts as arguments, the helper functions (one per distinct action, like move_file or categorize_by_extension), the decision tree mapping each input type to an output action, how errors are caught and logged, and what the dry-run mode will show versus a real run. If any of these are missing, ask for them explicitly before approving the design.

**How to review the decision tree.** Copy your rules from Step 2 and check them one by one against Claude's design. For a file organizer: does Claude's logic handle .pdf? .PDF? .Pdf? Extension case sensitivity is one of the most common first-agent bugs. Does it handle files with multiple dots in the name (report.final.v2.pdf)? Does it handle hidden files starting with a dot (.DS_Store)? Check every edge case you wrote down. If one is missing, tell Claude: *You did not address [edge case]. Add a rule: [what should happen].*

**Questions to ask during design review.** Ask Claude: *What happens if the source folder does not exist?* A well-designed agent catches this before entering the main loop and exits with a clear error message. Ask: *What happens if a file is open by another program and cannot be moved?* The agent should log the failure and continue rather than crash. Ask: *How does the dry-run mode work — does it show exactly what would have happened, or just a count?* It should show exactly what would happen, with full file paths.

**The prompt for adding the no-delete rule.** Tell Claude: *Add one hard constraint to the design: this agent must never call any delete function on any file or directory under any circumstances. If a file needs to be removed from view, it should be moved to a designated review folder, not deleted. Confirm this rule is in the design before we proceed.*

**When to approve the design.** Approve when every edge case is addressed, the no-delete rule is confirmed, you understand what each function does without needing to read code, and the dry-run mode is described clearly. If something is unclear, ask Claude to explain it in plain English. Understanding the design is your primary protection against surprises during testing.
steps.done_when: Claude has shown you the design and you have corrected it until it matches your loop and edge cases.

steps.title: Build it and test on safe data
steps.body:
Tell Claude: *Build the complete agent as a script. Include all tools, the main loop, logging for every action, and a --dry-run flag that previews without making changes.*

Once the script is written, ask Claude to create a **test folder with sample files** that mirror your real data. Never test against your real Downloads folder on the first run.

Run it in dry-run mode first. Read the log. Did it plan to do what you expected? Did it handle the edge cases you defined? If yes, run it for real against the test folder.

When something is wrong, describe the symptom precisely. Name what worked and what did not. *PDFs sorted correctly but .JPG images went to Other instead of Images. Check the extension matching, it might be case-sensitive.* Do not say *fix the bug* with no direction.

**The exact prompt to build.** Use this: *Build the complete agent script now. Requirements: (1) main loop processes every item in the source folder, (2) logging writes every action to logs/agent_run_[timestamp].log with a timestamp per line, (3) --dry-run flag runs the full logic but writes "[DRY RUN] would move X to Y" instead of actually moving anything, (4) end-of-run summary prints total processed, total moved, total skipped, and any warnings. Make the script runnable with: python agent.py --source ./test-data --dry-run*

**How to create good test data.** Ask Claude: *Create a test-data folder with at least 15 sample files that cover every case in the decision tree: at least two of each extension type we handle, one file with no extension, one file with a name that has multiple dots, one hidden file starting with a dot, and one file with a very long name. List exactly what you created and what category each one should end up in.* Read that list. It becomes your expected output — the checklist you verify against during dry-run.

**Reading the dry-run log.** Open the log file Claude created. Go through it line by line. For each line, check: does the source file match a file in your test-data list? Does the destination match the expected category from your checklist? If a file is missing from the log, the agent skipped it without logging — ask Claude to fix the logging before testing again. If a file went to the wrong destination, describe the mismatch: *test_report.FINAL.pdf was categorized as Other instead of Documents. The extension matching is treating .FINAL as the extension instead of .pdf.*

**Running real against test data.** Only after dry-run looks correct: run without the flag. Then open the destination folders and verify files are where the log said they would be. Check that the test-data folder is now empty (or has only files that should be skipped). Check the logs folder for the new run file. A successful real run on test data is the green light to move to hardening.
steps.done_when: Your agent has completed one full run on test data and the log shows it behaved correctly.

steps.title: Harden it for real data
steps.body:
Test data never covers every real-world weirdness. Before you point your agent at the real target, add the three features that turn a script into a tool:

1. **Verbose logging** so you can see every decision and every action.
2. **A dry-run mode** so you can preview a run without committing to it.
3. **A summary at the end** with counts, warnings, and any files the agent flagged.

Now do the belt-and-suspenders run: back up your real target (copy the folder, snapshot the data). Run dry-run against it. Read the log. If the plan looks right, run it for real. If not, update the rules and dry-run again.

**The hardening checklist.** Before running against real data, ask Claude to verify all six hardening items: *Review the agent script and confirm: (1) every file operation is wrapped in a try-except block that logs the error and continues rather than crashing, (2) the agent checks that the source folder exists before starting and exits cleanly if it does not, (3) all logs are written with timestamps in ISO format, (4) the end-of-run summary includes a count of errors and the names of any files that could not be processed, (5) there is no delete call anywhere in the code, (6) the script works correctly if run twice on the same source folder — it does not double-process files that were already moved.* If any item fails the check, ask Claude to fix it before proceeding.

**The backup step.** Do not skip this. Tell Claude: *Give me the exact terminal command to create a backup of my real target folder before the agent runs. I want a timestamped copy so I can restore if needed.* Run that command. Verify the backup exists and has the right number of files. A backup that takes thirty seconds to create has saved entire projects.

**The real dry-run.** Run the agent in dry-run mode against your real target. The log will be longer than the test-data log. Read the first twenty lines and the last twenty lines. Look for: files categorized as Other that you think should go elsewhere (update the rules), warnings about files the agent could not read, and any files that appear twice in the log (a sign of a loop bug). Address every warning before running for real.

**After the real run.** Spot-check the destination folders. Pick five random files from the log and verify they are where the log says. Check the Review folder — any files there are ones the agent could not categorize and flagged for your attention. These are real edge cases your rules did not cover. Add rules for the common ones and leave the truly weird ones in Review. That is the right behavior.

**The prompt to add a run-twice safety check.** Tell Claude: *Add logic so the agent skips any file that already exists at the destination with the same name and size. Log these as "already processed — skipped" instead of trying to overwrite. This makes the agent safe to run multiple times on the same source folder.*
steps.done_when: Your agent has run successfully on your real target and you have a log file you can read.

steps.title: Make it easy to run again
steps.body:
One-off scripts get lost. A tool you can run again is infrastructure.

Ask Claude: *Create a short command or shell alias so I can run the agent in one line. Dry-run should be a flag. Default to my real target folder.* Save the command somewhere you will find it (a README, a note, a shell config).

You just built an autonomous agent. Same pattern as every AI agent making headlines: perceive, decide, act, repeat. The tools change, the loop does not. Next level: give agents APIs. The level after: agents that coordinate with other agents. The loop stays the same. You are an agent builder now.

**The three things to save.** The one-liner command. The location of the log folder. The rule list — the edge-case rules you wrote in Step 2 and refined during testing. If you change the agent later, you will want to know what rules existed before so you can update them without losing the logic that already works.

**The prompt for a one-liner alias.** Tell Claude: *Write a shell alias named "run-agent" that runs the agent against my real target folder with verbose logging. Add a "run-agent-dry" alias that runs it in dry-run mode. Show me where to add these aliases in my shell config file and how to activate them without restarting the terminal.*

**Write a README.** Ask Claude: *Write a README.md for this agent that includes: what it does in one sentence, how to run it (the one-liner), what the flags do, where the logs go, the complete list of decision rules, and what to do if a file ends up in the wrong place.* A README you write today is the instruction manual you read six months from now when you have forgotten everything.

**What to schedule next.** The most natural upgrade for most agents is scheduling. Ask Claude: *How would I run this agent automatically every weekday morning? Give me the cron command for macOS and the Task Scheduler steps for Windows.* You do not have to implement it now, but knowing the path makes the agent feel more like a real tool.

**The bigger picture.** Every production agent in industry — from customer support bots to trading systems to content pipelines — runs some version of the loop you just built: perceive (read inputs), decide (apply rules), act (execute actions), repeat (loop until done). The tools change. The languages change. The loop does not. What you built today is not a toy. It is the foundation pattern. The agents making headlines are this loop, scaled, with better tools, and more sophisticated rules. You understand the foundation now.
steps.done_when: You can run your agent with one command and you know where that command is saved.
